claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 15 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started with your Child Benefit eligibility assessment, I need to ask some questions about your child (or children).

**First question: How old is your child?**

The age of the child is important because:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** typically don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children, let me know about each of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I've got three kids actually. Alex is 4, Charlie is 15, and Blake is 16. I want to make sure I'm getting everything I'm entitled to for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great that you're checking your entitlement for all three children. Let me work through each of them:

**Your children:**
- **Alex** (age 4)
- **Charlie** (age 15) 
- **Blake** (age 16)

Let me start by checking the eligibility for each child based on their age.

For **Alex (age 4)** and **Charlie (age 15)** - both are under 16, so they would generally qualify. However, I need to check one important condition first:

**Are either Alex or Charlie working full-time (16 hours or more per week)?**

For **Blake (age 16)** - since Blake is 16-19 years old, there are additional requirements:

**Is Blake currently in approved education or training?** This could include:
- Full-time non-advanced education (like A-levels, Scottish Highers, or equivalent)
- Approved training programs

Let me know about their work/education status and I'll be able to give you a complete assessment for all three children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No, Alex and Charlie aren't working at all - they're just at school doing their normal schoolwork.

As for Blake, well, they actually left school recently but just signed up with the local careers service to figure out what to do next. They're not in college or anything like that right now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that clarification! Let me work through the eligibility for each child:

**Alex (age 4) and Charlie (age 15):** Since they're both under 16 and not working full-time, they meet the basic qualifying child criteria. ✓

**Blake (age 16):** This situation needs careful consideration. Blake has left school and is not currently in approved education or training. However, there's an important "extension period" that may apply.

Let me check the specific rules for Blake's situation:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! For Blake, there's potentially an "extension period" that applies. Let me get more details:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news for Blake! There's a special **20-week extension period** that may apply. Let me check the specific requirements:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Here's the key information for Blake:

**For Blake (age 16):** There's a **20-week extension period** available for young people aged 16-17 who have:
1. Left approved education or training
2. Registered with a qualifying service (such as a government careers service, Connexions, or armed services)

**Important questions about Blake:**

1. **When exactly did Blake leave school?** (I need to know if it's been less than 20 weeks)
2. **Has Blake registered with the local careers service?** (You mentioned they "just signed up" - this is great!)

If Blake left school within the last 20 weeks AND has registered with the careers service, then the extension period applies! The extension would:
- Last for 20 weeks from when they left education, OR
- Until Blake turns 18, whichever comes first

Could you tell me approximately when Blake left school?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake left school about 3 months ago, so that should be well within the 20 weeks. And yes, they've registered with the careers service - I made sure they did that straight away.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! That's really good that you helped Blake register right away. Let me confirm the timeline:

**Blake left school about 3 months ago (approximately 12 weeks)** - this is well within the 20-week extension period, and Blake has registered with the careers service. ✓

Now I need to check one more important condition for Blake:

**Is Blake working full-time?** (Full-time is defined as 24 hours or more per week)

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
So for Blake specifically: **Is Blake working 24 hours or more per week?** 

(Just to clarify - part-time work under 24 hours per week is fine and wouldn't affect eligibility)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't working at all at the moment. They're just trying to figure out what they want to do next.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's helpful to know. Now I need to check a few more conditions that apply to all three children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Are any of your children (Alex, Charlie, or Blake) receiving benefits in their own right?** 

This would include things like:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Income Support

(This is quite rare for children, but I need to check)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them are receiving any benefits in their own names. They're all just living at home with me - well, except for Blake who doesn't live with me anymore, but they're not getting any benefits themselves.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's important information! You mentioned that **Blake doesn't live with you anymore**. This is a key factor for Child Benefit eligibility.

Before I continue with the other checks, I need to understand Blake's living situation better:

**Are you contributing to Blake's upkeep or maintenance?** For example:
- Do you provide regular financial support to Blake?
- Do you pay for their living costs, food, clothing, etc.?
- Or are they fully supporting themselves or being supported by someone else?

This matters because Child Benefit can still be claimed for a child who doesn't live with you, **if** you're contributing at least the amount of Child Benefit to their upkeep.

Also, for Alex and Charlie who do live with you - they are living with you full-time, correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, Alex and Charlie live with me full-time at home.

For Blake, yes, I do help out financially. I give them money regularly to help with things like clothes, travel, and just general living costs - it's about £50 a week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that detail. The £50 per week you're giving Blake is important - I'll need to check if that meets the contribution requirement, but first let me continue gathering information about all the children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Next question:

**Are any of your children in an apprenticeship?**

This includes:
- Any type of apprenticeship in England (Intermediate, Advanced, Higher, or Degree Apprenticeships)
- Modern Apprenticeships in Scotland
- Apprenticeships in Wales (except Foundation Apprenticeships, which are approved)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them are doing apprenticeships. Alex and Charlie are too young, and Blake isn't doing anything like that at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now I need to check your eligibility as the claimant. Let me move forward:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status:

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

(If yes, this is the simplest path. If your situation is more complex - such as if you have pre-settled or settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or you're a Crown servant abroad - let me know and I can check those specific paths too.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Now let me check about your responsibility for the children:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now let's confirm responsibility for each child:

**For Alex and Charlie:** Since they live with you full-time, you clearly meet the responsibility requirement. ✓

**For Blake:** You mentioned you contribute £50 per week. To meet the responsibility requirement, you need to contribute at least the amount of Child Benefit. Let me check the current rates:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
The specific Child Benefit rates aren't in my specification, but based on current UK government rates (as of 2024), Child Benefit is approximately:
- **£25.60 per week** for the eldest/only child
- **£16.95 per week** for additional children

Since you're contributing **£50 per week to Blake**, this is well above the Child Benefit amount, so you meet the responsibility requirement for Blake. ✓

Now let me check the next requirement:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Important question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for any of your children (Alex, Charlie, or Blake)?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Someone else Blake might be living with

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Charlie since they live with me.

For Blake, they don't live with me anymore, but nobody else is claiming for them either. I'm still the one helping out financially.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's clear. Now let me check about where the children are located:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check if any special circumstances apply to your children's living situations:

**Are any of your children:**
1. **In hospital** for an extended period?
2. **In local authority care** (looked after by the council)?
3. **Being fostered** by someone else?

Or are they all living in normal circumstances (Alex and Charlie with you at home, and Blake living independently)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Well, Alex has actually been in hospital for a while now - about 3 months. But I still visit and buy things for them like clothes and magazines and their favorite snacks.

Charlie is just at home with me normally, nothing special there.

And Blake is living independently, not in hospital or care or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's important information about Alex. Let me check the hospital duration rules:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Alex has been in hospital for about 3 months (approximately 12 weeks). This is right at the threshold. Let me ask some clarifying questions:

**For Alex's hospital stay:**

1. **Has Alex been in hospital continuously for 3 months, or has there been a break?** If there were any breaks, how long were they?

2. **Are you regularly spending money on Alex's needs?** (You mentioned visiting and buying clothes, magazines, and snacks - this is good!)

The rules state that Child Benefit can continue for up to 12 weeks if a child is in hospital. If the stay exceeds 12 weeks, you may still be eligible if you're regularly spending money on the child's needs (which you are doing).

Can you tell me more precisely how long Alex has been in hospital - is it exactly 12 weeks, or slightly more or less?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been exactly 12 weeks - so right at that 3 month mark. And yes, I've been spending money on them the whole time - buying clothes, magazines, their favorite foods. I want to make sure they have what they need while they're in there.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that clarification. Since Alex has been in hospital for exactly 12 weeks (right at the threshold) and you've been regularly spending money on their needs throughout, let me check the next part:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Since you **are** regularly spending money on Alex (buying clothes, magazines, and snacks), this meets the requirement. Even though Alex is at the 12-week mark, you qualify for continued Child Benefit because you're actively contributing to their needs. ✓

Now let me continue with the final checks:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Final question for all three children:

**Is the local council paying towards any of your children's accommodation or maintenance?**

This would include if the council is:
- Paying for Alex's hospital accommodation or care
- Paying for Charlie's living costs
- Paying for Blake's accommodation or living expenses

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying for any of them. I'm covering all their costs myself - whether it's Alex in hospital, Charlie at home, or the money I give to Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Thank you for providing all that information. Let me now complete the assessment by checking the final outcome:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now let me provide you with the complete assessment for all three children:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit for All Three Children!

Here's the summary for each of your children:

---

### **Alex (age 4) - ELIGIBLE ✓**
Even though Alex has been in hospital for 12 weeks (right at the threshold), you remain eligible because you're **regularly spending money on their needs** - the clothes, magazines, and snacks you've been buying. This is an important exception to the 12-week hospital rule.

### **Charlie (age 15) - ELIGIBLE ✓**
Charlie qualifies straightforwardly as a child under 16 who lives with you at home and isn't working full-time.

### **Blake (age 16) - ELIGIBLE ✓**
Blake qualifies under the **special 20-week extension period** for 16-17 year olds who have:
- Left education within the last 20 weeks (Blake left 3 months ago ✓)
- Registered with a careers service (Blake has done this ✓)
- Not working 24+ hours per week (Blake isn't working ✓)

Even though Blake doesn't live with you, your **£50 per week contribution** well exceeds the Child Benefit amount, so you meet the responsibility requirement.

---

## **Next Steps:**

1. **Apply for Child Benefit** (if you haven't already):
   - Apply online at: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim
   - Or by post using form CH2

2. **Documents you'll need:**
   - Proof of identity for yourself
   - Birth certificates or adoption certificates for each child

3. **Important timing note for Blake:**
   - Blake's eligibility under the extension period will last for **20 weeks from when they left school** (so you have about 8 weeks remaining in this extension period)
   - After that, Blake would need to either:
     - Start approved education/training, OR
     - The extension would end

4. **High Income Child Benefit Charge:**
   - If you or your partner earn over £60,000, you may need to pay back some of the Child Benefit through your tax return
   - This doesn't affect your eligibility - you can still claim

5. **Payments:**
   - Child Benefit is paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

---

**Do you have any questions about claiming or your eligibility?**

reasonings: Alex (age 4) is eligible for Child Benefit. Although Alex is under 16 and not working full-time, they have been in hospital for exactly 12 weeks. However, you are regularly spending money on Alex's needs (clothes, magazines, snacks), which meets the exception to the 12-week hospital rule. Alex is not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, and the local council is not paying for their care. You meet residency requirements, have responsibility for Alex, and no one else is claiming for them.

Charlie (age 15) is eligible for Child Benefit. Charlie is under 16, not working full-time, attending normal school, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. Charlie lives with you full-time at home in normal circumstances. You meet UK residency requirements, have full responsibility for Charlie, and no one else is claiming for them. The local council is not paying for Charlie's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 16) is eligible for Child Benefit under the 20-week extension period. Blake left school approximately 3 months ago (12 weeks), which is within the 20-week extension period for 16-17 year olds who have left education and registered with a qualifying careers service. Blake has registered with the local careers service. Blake is not working (under 24 hours/week threshold), not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. Although Blake does not live with you, you are contributing £50 per week to their upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount and meets the responsibility requirement. You meet UK residency requirements, no one else is claiming for Blake, Blake is living in normal circumstances (not in hospital/care), and the local council is not paying for Blake's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for all three children: Alex (4), Charlie (15), and Blake (16). Alex qualifies despite the 12-week hospital stay because the claimant regularly spends money on their needs. Charlie qualifies as a standard under-16 qualifying child living at home. Blake qualifies under the special 20-week extension period for 16-17 year olds who have left education and registered with careers services, with the claimant meeting the responsibility requirement through financial contributions of £50/week despite Blake living independently. The claimant meets all UK residency requirements and has sole responsibility for all three children with no competing claims.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Has the child (aged 16-17) left education within the last 20 weeks AND registered with a qualifying service?
response.help_text: 20-week extension available if child has left approved education/training and registered with: government careers service, Connexions, or armed services. Extension ends at week 20 or when child turns 18, whichever is sooner.

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 4) is eligible for Child Benefit. Although Alex is under 16 and not working full-time, they have been in hospital for exactly 12 weeks. However, you are regularly spending money on Alex's needs (clothes, magazines, snacks), which meets the exception to the 12-week hospital rule. Alex is not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, and the local council is not paying for their care. You meet residency requirements, have responsibility for Alex, and no one else is claiming for them.

Charlie (age 15) is eligible for Child Benefit. Charlie is under 16, not working full-time, attending normal school, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. Charlie lives with you full-time at home in normal circumstances. You meet UK residency requirements, have full responsibility for Charlie, and no one else is claiming for them. The local council is not paying for Charlie's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 16) is eligible for Child Benefit under the 20-week extension period. Blake left school approximately 3 months ago (12 weeks), which is within the 20-week extension period for 16-17 year olds who have left education and registered with a qualifying careers service. Blake has registered with the local careers service. Blake is not working (under 24 hours/week threshold), not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. Although Blake does not live with you, you are contributing £50 per week to their upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount and meets the responsibility requirement. You meet UK residency requirements, no one else is claiming for Blake, Blake is living in normal circumstances (not in hospital/care), and the local council is not paying for Blake's accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for all three children: Alex (4), Charlie (15), and Blake (16). Alex qualifies despite the 12-week hospital stay because the claimant regularly spends money on their needs. Charlie qualifies as a standard under-16 qualifying child living at home. Blake qualifies under the special 20-week extension period for 16-17 year olds who have left education and registered with careers services, with the claimant meeting the responsibility requirement through financial contributions of £50/week despite Blake living independently. The claimant meets all UK residency requirements and has sole responsibility for all three children with no competing claims.
